question_text: Before an RCFE admission date is confirmed, why should the administrator review appraisal information, physician documentation, and facility capability together?
answer_a: The combined review shows whether identified needs can be matched with documented services before move-in.
answer_b: It allows the facility to delay all records until after the resident arrives.
answer_c: It replaces the need to communicate with the resident or responsible person.
answer_d: It proves that every future change in condition can be managed without review.
rationale: Admission decisions should be based on connected evidence showing resident needs, service capacity, and unresolved items before the facility accepts responsibility.
core_category: Resident Admission, Retention, and Assessment Procedures

question_text: What does source control mean during admission screening?
answer_a: Using only verbal family statements because they are easiest to collect.
answer_b: Separating what was reported, observed, documented, and still pending so the record remains clear.
answer_c: Discarding informal notes once the admission agreement is signed.
answer_d: Treating every document as current even when dates or signatures are missing.
rationale: Source control helps the administrator distinguish reliable records from pending or secondhand information during admission review.
core_category: Resident Admission, Retention, and Assessment Procedures

question_text: Which needs and services plan statement gives staff the clearest direction?
answer_a: Assist the resident when staff think help may be useful.
answer_b: Monitor the resident and keep everyone comfortable.
answer_c: Offer a meal cue at lunch and dinner; notify the administrator after two meal refusals in seven days.
answer_d: Provide good care and update the plan later if someone asks.
rationale: A staff-ready instruction identifies the service, timing, observation point, and notification threshold instead of relying on vague judgment.
core_category: Resident Admission, Retention, and Assessment Procedures

question_text: Which learner submission would violate the course privacy standard?
answer_a: A fictional memo using Sample Resident A.
answer_b: A general description of a pending physician report.
answer_c: A role label such as responsible person or outside provider.
answer_d: A real resident name paired with a real facility incident.
rationale: Course activities must use fictional or de-identified learning examples and must not include real resident, facility, staff, or protected information.
core_category: Resident Admission, Retention, and Assessment Procedures

question_text: Which factor belongs in a facility capability review before admission?
answer_a: Whether staffing, environment, training, and procedures can support the assessed needs.
answer_b: Whether the room should be filled quickly to avoid vacancy.
answer_c: Whether the family prefers fewer questions before move-in.
answer_d: Whether the resident seems pleasant during a brief tour.
rationale: Capability review considers operational ability to deliver documented services across shifts, not convenience or general impressions.
core_category: Resident Admission, Retention, and Assessment Procedures

question_text: What does Draft / Pending CDSS/ACS Approval status require in Moodle?
answer_a: The course may issue certificates as soon as learners pass the post-test.
answer_b: The course must remain labeled as pending and certificate release must stay blocked until approval metadata and evidence gates are satisfied.
answer_c: The instructor may remove completion tracking while approval is pending.
answer_d: The learner may count the course as approved credit before official metadata exists.
rationale: Pending status requires clear labeling and certificate controls so Moodle does not imply approval or release credit prematurely.
core_category: Resident Admission, Retention, and Assessment Procedures

question_text: Which documentation phrase best marks an unresolved admission item?
answer_a: Everything appears fine.
answer_b: Family says there are no concerns.
answer_c: Physician report requested on the review date; admission decision remains pending until received and compared with appraisal findings.
answer_d: No need to document this because it should arrive later.
rationale: Pending items should be dated, named, and connected to the decision they affect so the admission record stays reviewable.
core_category: Resident Admission, Retention, and Assessment Procedures

question_text: A responsible person disagrees with the facility's request for more pre-admission information. What is the best administrator response?
answer_a: Accept the admission immediately to avoid conflict.
answer_b: Tell staff to collect the information after move-in without recording the gap.
answer_c: Ignore the disagreement because family communication is not part of admission screening.
answer_d: Explain the needed information, document the pending item, and continue review through the approved process.
rationale: Respectful communication and objective documentation help resolve admission gaps without bypassing required review.
core_category: Resident Admission, Retention, and Assessment Procedures

question_text: Why should admission notes avoid personality labels?
answer_a: Objective notes focus on observable facts, actions, notifications, pending items, and follow-up.
answer_b: Personality labels are required for every admission record.
answer_c: Labels make it easier to skip the needs and services plan.
answer_d: Subjective wording eliminates the need for reappraisal.
rationale: Objective language supports continuity, dignity, and compliance review because later readers can see facts rather than assumptions.
core_category: Resident Admission, Retention, and Assessment Procedures

question_text: What does admission-agreement linkage require?
answer_a: The admission agreement should be kept separate from assessed needs.
answer_b: The services and expectations in the agreement should align with the facility's documented ability to meet assessed needs.
answer_c: The agreement should promise that the resident's condition will not change.
answer_d: The agreement can be completed before any appraisal-related information is reviewed.
rationale: The admission agreement should not stand apart from appraisal findings and service capability; it should reflect a supportable admission decision.
core_category: Resident Admission, Retention, and Assessment Procedures

question_text: Staff report that the current plan says only 'assist as needed' after repeated evening confusion. Which revision best improves the plan?
answer_a: Continue using the same phrase because it gives staff flexibility.
answer_b: Delete staff notes until the resident asks for more help.
answer_c: Provide an evening orientation cue at 7 p.m.; document response; notify the administrator after three episodes in one week.
answer_d: Tell family that staff will handle it without further review.
rationale: The revised instruction links the observed pattern to a specific action, documentation expectation, and notification threshold.
core_category: Resident Admission, Retention, and Assessment Procedures

question_text: A resident has two falls and new missed meals after admission. Which administrator action best reflects reappraisal discipline?
answer_a: Wait for the annual review because the resident was already admitted.
answer_b: Ask a caregiver to remember the concern without changing records.
answer_c: Assume the family will call if the pattern becomes serious.
answer_d: Review the record, identify the change pattern, update the plan if needed, and document follow-up.
rationale: Repeated changes after admission should prompt record review and possible plan updates rather than informal monitoring alone.
core_category: Resident Admission, Retention, and Assessment Procedures

question_text: A prospective resident declines to provide a requested document during screening. What should the administrator document?
answer_a: The document requested, the refusal or delay, who was notified, the impact on admission review, and the next step.
answer_b: Only that the resident was uncooperative.
answer_c: Nothing, because declined documents cannot be mentioned.
answer_d: A promise that admission will proceed regardless of missing information.
rationale: A neutral note preserves dignity while explaining the missing record and its effect on the admission decision.
core_category: Resident Admission, Retention, and Assessment Procedures

question_text: The physician report is received but conflicts with family statements about transfer assistance. What should happen before final admission approval?
answer_a: Choose the version that makes admission easiest.
answer_b: Clarify the conflict through approved channels, document the discrepancy, and compare the resolved information with facility capability.
answer_c: Ignore the report because family statements are more recent.
answer_d: Ask staff to solve the issue after move-in.
rationale: Conflicting admission information must be resolved or clearly tracked before the facility relies on it for a fit decision.
core_category: Resident Admission, Retention, and Assessment Procedures

question_text: A family requests immediate move-in, but the appraisal is incomplete. Which response best protects the process?
answer_a: Schedule the move-in and complete the appraisal if time allows.
answer_b: Tell the family the paperwork does not matter if the room is available.
answer_c: Explain that the admission decision remains pending until required review is completed and documented.
answer_d: Ask direct-care staff to make the admission decision.
rationale: The administrator should maintain the admission workflow even when external pressure favors a faster move-in.
core_category: Resident Admission, Retention, and Assessment Procedures

question_text: After a plan update, which follow-up entry is most useful for audit review?
answer_a: Plan changed, no other details.
answer_b: Resident is better now.
answer_c: Staff know what to do.
answer_d: Plan updated to add dinner cueing; staff notified on the review date; administrator will recheck meal records in seven days.
rationale: Audit-ready follow-up names the change, notification, and review timeline so the record can be understood later.
core_category: Resident Admission, Retention, and Assessment Procedures

question_text: A record audit shows the service plan has not been updated since admission despite three documented changes. What is the appropriate administrator priority?
answer_a: Bring the plan current by reviewing the changes, updating instructions, documenting notifications, and setting follow-up.
answer_b: Leave the plan unchanged because the resident has not complained.
answer_c: Remove older notes so the file looks consistent.
answer_d: Wait for a licensing visit to identify needed corrections.
rationale: A plan that no longer matches the record should be corrected through review, updated instructions, and documented follow-up.
core_category: Resident Admission, Retention, and Assessment Procedures

question_text: When does a retention concern require escalation beyond routine staff action?
answer_a: Whenever a family asks a question about services.
answer_b: When repeated facts suggest the resident's needs may exceed current facility capability or approved procedures.
answer_c: Only after the resident has moved out.
answer_d: Never, because retention decisions are informal.
rationale: Escalation is appropriate when the issue exceeds routine instructions and requires leadership, outside coordination, or higher-level review.
core_category: Resident Admission, Retention, and Assessment Procedures

question_text: A resident prefers independence, but staff observe a new pattern of missed meals. Which documentation approach best preserves dignity and supports review?
answer_a: Write that the resident is stubborn and refuses help.
answer_b: Record only the family opinion and skip staff observations.
answer_c: Document the observed pattern, the resident preference, the service offered, and the follow-up plan.
answer_d: State that independence is no longer allowed.
rationale: Balanced documentation can respect resident preference while still addressing objective changes that affect service planning.
core_category: Resident Admission, Retention, and Assessment Procedures

question_text: Which Moodle evidence should support completion for this course?
answer_a: A learner's verbal statement that the course was opened.
answer_b: Only the final quiz score, even if activities are missing.
answer_c: An email saying the learner intended to finish later.
answer_d: Page views, scenario completions, participation response, assignment submission, quiz pass, evaluation, and completion gate audit.
rationale: The filed course requires evidence across duration, activity completion, participation, assessment, evaluation, and certificate controls.
core_category: Resident Admission, Retention, and Assessment Procedures

question_text: Scenario: Sample Resident E wants admission after a hospital stay, but the record lacks current mobility information. What should the administrator do?
answer_a: Request and review current information, document the pending item, and determine whether facility services can meet the identified need.
answer_b: Accept admission because the resident previously lived independently.
answer_c: Assume the prior appraisal still answers all questions.
answer_d: Promise the family that no additional review will be needed.
rationale: Current mobility information affects admission fit, service planning, and staff instructions, so it must be reviewed before a supportable decision.
core_category: Resident Admission, Retention, and Assessment Procedures

question_text: Scenario: Sample Resident F's plan lists reminders, but staff now report repeated exit-seeking attempts. Which response is most appropriate?
answer_a: Tell staff to keep trying without changing the plan.
answer_b: Open a reappraisal review, update immediate instructions, escalate according to policy, and document the pattern.
answer_c: Delete the exit-seeking notes to avoid concern.
answer_d: Wait until the family requests a meeting.
rationale: A repeated exit-seeking pattern can indicate that the current plan no longer matches resident needs and requires structured review.
core_category: Resident Admission, Retention, and Assessment Procedures

question_text: Scenario: Sample Resident G's responsible person says the resident eats well, but meal records show five missed dinners in ten days. What should the administrator rely on?
answer_a: Only the responsible person's statement.
answer_b: Only staff memory with no record review.
answer_c: The documented pattern, follow-up communication, plan review, and any pending clarification.
answer_d: A general belief that appetite changes are not an administrator concern.
rationale: The administrator should reconcile reported information with documented patterns and update the plan when the record supports a trigger.
core_category: Resident Admission, Retention, and Assessment Procedures

question_text: Scenario: Sample Resident H's family demands a final relocation decision during a tense phone call. What is the best administrator response?
answer_a: Issue a final decision immediately to end the call.
answer_b: Avoid documenting the call because it was tense.
answer_c: Ask a caregiver to tell the family what will happen.
answer_d: Document the concern, explain that review will follow approved channels, escalate as needed, and preserve respectful communication.
rationale: Retention and relocation-related concerns should follow documented review and escalation rather than pressured decisions during conflict.
core_category: Resident Admission, Retention, and Assessment Procedures

question_text: Scenario: Sample Resident I has a missing physician report and an incomplete pre-admission appraisal. Which admission note is strongest?
answer_a: Admission review pending; physician report and appraisal completion requested; facility capability decision deferred until records are reviewed.
answer_b: Resident seems fine, so move-in is approved.
answer_c: Family wants admission soon, so missing records are not important.
answer_d: Staff can decide what services are needed after move-in.
rationale: The note clearly identifies pending records and links them to the admission decision instead of bypassing review.
core_category: Resident Admission, Retention, and Assessment Procedures

question_text: Scenario: Sample Resident J has new nighttime disorientation. Staff have three different informal routines. What should the administrator do?
answer_a: Allow each staff member to continue using personal preference.
answer_b: Create a consistent plan instruction, document the trigger, notify staff, and set a review date.
answer_c: Tell staff to stop documenting nighttime events.
answer_d: Assume the condition is temporary and take no action.
rationale: Inconsistent informal practice should be replaced with documented service instructions and follow-up tied to the observed change.
core_category: Resident Admission, Retention, and Assessment Procedures

question_text: Scenario: Sample Resident K's file contains admission notes, incident notes, and family emails, but no clear follow-up timeline. What audit correction is most useful?
answer_a: Remove family emails from the file.
answer_b: Write that the administrator reviewed everything.
answer_c: Add a dated follow-up plan that connects the trigger, action taken, responsible person notified, and next review date.
answer_d: Wait until the next annual update.
rationale: A clear follow-up timeline turns scattered records into a reviewable decision path.
core_category: Resident Admission, Retention, and Assessment Procedures

question_text: Scenario: Sample Resident L's family asks the learner to use a real incident in the Moodle assignment. What should the learner do?
answer_a: Use the incident because it will make the assignment realistic.
answer_b: Include the resident name but omit the facility name.
answer_c: Upload the real record as supporting evidence.
answer_d: Convert the issue into a fictional example with no identifying details.
rationale: The course requires fictional or de-identified work so Moodle submissions do not expose protected information.
core_category: Resident Admission, Retention, and Assessment Procedures

question_text: Scenario: Sample Resident M has repeated staff notes that current supervision may not be enough. What should a retention review memo include?
answer_a: Trigger pattern, records reviewed, current supports, capability concern, escalation path, and follow-up date.
answer_b: Only the administrator's conclusion with no supporting facts.
answer_c: A statement blaming the resident for needing more help.
answer_d: A promise that no relocation discussion will ever occur.
rationale: A retention review memo should organize the evidence and next steps without blame or unsupported guarantees.
core_category: Resident Admission, Retention, and Assessment Procedures

question_text: Scenario: Sample Resident N's service-plan update is complete, but the post-test and evaluation are unfinished. What should Moodle show?
answer_a: Course complete because the plan activity was submitted.
answer_b: Incomplete until required activities, quiz pass, evaluation, and completion gate conditions are satisfied.
answer_c: Certificate released because the learner spent time in the course.
answer_d: No evidence needed if the instructor remembers the learner participating.
rationale: Completion depends on all required course gates, not a single activity, and certificate controls remain tied to evidence and approval metadata.
core_category: Resident Admission, Retention, and Assessment Procedures
